title: Catechist's Guide — The Seven Petitions
html:
Catechist's Guide

For class preparation and delivery. For the catechist's use only.

Class Summary

Estimated duration: 60 minutes

Central theme: The seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer form a complete map of the Christian life: three launch us toward God (his Name, his Kingdom, his Will) and four open us to receive his mercy (bread, forgiveness, protection, deliverance).

Objectives:

  • Know and understand each of the seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer.
  • Distinguish the three God-centered petitions ("Thy") from the four us-centered petitions ("Us").
  • Understand the demand of the fifth petition on forgiveness.
  • Apply each petition to daily life.
  • Treasure the Lord's Prayer as the prayer that contains everything.

Key verse: «Pray then in this way: Our Father...» (Mt 6:9-13)

CCC references: 2803-2865

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Big Question 8 min Ask: «What are we really asking for in each petition?» Tell the story of Saint Augustine in Hippo. Introduce the idea of seven jewels.
2. The three petitions to God 15 min Explain each one: hallowing the Name (CCC 2807), thy Kingdom come (CCC 2816), thy will be done (CCC 2822). Read Mt 26:39 in Gethsemane.
3. The four petitions for us 15 min Bread: material and Eucharistic. Forgiveness: special emphasis on the terrible and wonderful condition (Mt 6:14-15). Temptation and deliverance from evil.
4. Biblical connection 7 min Read Mt 6:9-13 in full. Connect with Eph 3:18-19 and Jn 17:15. Share quotes from saints (Thérèse, Carlo Acutis).
5. For Reflection 10 min Groups of 2-3. Each person shares which petition they find hardest to live. Then share as a group.
6. Closing 5 min Read the Key Idea. Pray the Lord's Prayer very slowly, with pauses between each petition.

Notes for the Catechist

The Lord's Prayer as a spiritual map

Many have prayed the Our Father thousands of times without pausing to think about each petition. The goal of this class is not to teach something new, but to make conscious what they already pray. Help your students move from automatic recitation to conscious prayer. A good image: the Our Father is a treasure chest with seven jewels; most people carry it without opening it. This class is opening it together.

The 3 + 4 structure

The pattern is pedagogically powerful: first God (three petitions with "thy"), then us (four petitions with "us"). It is the order of love: first adore, then ask. It is also the order of happiness: those who put God first receive everything else as well (Mt 6:33). It is worth drawing or projecting this simple structure: 3 ↑ 4 ↓.

The fifth petition: forgiveness

This petition is often the most impactful part of the class, because it touches real wounds. Be prepared for emotions to surface. Some will share that they struggle to forgive a parent, an ex-spouse, a friend who betrayed them. Do not minimize their pain. But remember that Christian forgiveness is not sentimentality: it is an act of the will assisted by grace. We do not forgive because "it no longer hurts," but because Christ first forgave us. Quote Colossians 3:13: "Forgive one another as the Lord has forgiven you." A pastoral suggestion: recommend that someone who is deeply hurting pray three Our Fathers for that person over the course of a week. The first day will seem impossible. By the third day, something begins to shift.

God's will and fear

Some students may have a distorted image of God as someone who wants to ruin their lives. If someone expresses fear of God's will, respond with tenderness: God is a Father, not a torturer. His will is our happiness. Ask: "What father wants evil for his child?" God's will is not a whim; it is a plan of love.

Bread, temptation, and daily life

Connect the fourth petition with daily trust. Ask: "What are you afraid will be lacking tomorrow?" For the sixth petition, make very clear that God does not tempt; trial can come from God for our growth, temptation comes from the devil, the world, or the flesh. Discerning the difference is key.

Age Adaptation

🌱 Seed (ages 7-12)

Use concrete images for each petition: "Hallowed be thy name" = a shiny crown for God; "daily bread" = a plate of food; "forgive us our trespasses" = two children holding hands after a fight. Activity: draw the seven petitions in seven squares. Memorize the full Lord's Prayer, word for word. Sing it if a simple musical version is available. Explain each petition by asking: "What are you saying to Daddy God with this phrase?"

🌿 Sprouts (ages 13-17)

Focus on forgiveness and temptation, because these are the struggles proper to this age: falling out with friends, peer pressure, temptations regarding purity, social media, identity. Opening question: "If you had to cross out one petition of the Lord's Prayer because you do not live it, which would it be?" Propose a challenge: pray the Lord's Prayer slowly every night for a week, writing in a phone note which petition resonated most that day.

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

These adults may have prayed the Lord's Prayer as children but abandoned the faith. Help them rediscover it as adults. Emphasize the fourth petition (material and Eucharistic bread) as a bridge to talk about the Mass. The fifth petition is also key: many carry guilt or resentments they have not worked through. Offer the possibility of confession as a concrete fulfillment of this petition.

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Go deeper into the theology of each petition using the Catechism directly (CCC 2807-2854). Explore the patristic sources: Saint Cyprian on Eucharistic bread, Saint Augustine (Letter to Proba) on the Lord's Prayer as the summary of all prayer, Tertullian and his commentary on the Lord's Prayer. Connect with the Liturgy: every Mass culminates with the Lord's Prayer; how does the assembly pray it? With reverence or in haste? The seventh petition and the prayer for peace that immediately follows in the Mass.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 157-165 («What do the seven petitions mean?»)
  • Saint Augustine, Epistle 130 to Proba: Classic text on prayer; Augustine shows how every true prayer is already in the Lord's Prayer.
  • Saint Cyprian of Carthage, "De Dominica Oratione": Complete patristic commentary on the Lord's Prayer, very accessible and pastoral.
  • Benedict XVI, "Jesus of Nazareth" (vol. 1): Chapter on the Lord's Prayer, with rich exegesis and theology.
  • Word on Fire: Bishop Robert Barron's commentary on the petitions of the Lord's Prayer.
  • Video: «The Lord's Prayer Explained» on YouTube (Ascension Presents or The Bible Project).
  • James Tissot: The "Le Pater Noster" series available on the Brooklyn Museum website; excellent for projecting images.

Difficult Questions

«If God already knows what I need, why should I ask?»

God knows what you need, but he wants you to ask him. Why? Because asking educates the heart. When a small child asks something of their dad, the dad already knew they were hungry, but he likes that the child trusts and comes to him. Prayer does not inform God; it transforms us. Asking for daily bread makes us humble and trusting. Asking for forgiveness makes us acknowledge our guilt. Asking to be delivered from evil reminds us of our fragility.

«I cannot forgive. They abused me. It is impossible.»

Christian forgiveness does not deny the gravity of the harm or the demands of justice. Forgiving is not saying "nothing happened" nor giving up on justice. It is handing the judgment over to God. It is ceasing to wish evil on the other person. For victims of serious abuse, the path of forgiveness can take years and requires professional and spiritual accompaniment. It cannot be forced; it must be asked for as grace. A first small step might be: "Lord, I cannot forgive this person. Give me even the desire to want to forgive them." God does not demand the impossible; he gives the grace for what he asks.

«Jesus taught us to say 'lead us not into temptation,' but James says God tempts no one. Is there a contradiction?»

There is no contradiction. The petition in Greek literally says "do not bring us into testing" (peirasmon). But in the Jewish context, God permits testing for our growth, not for our downfall. The petition is: "Do not allow us to enter a situation where we will succumb." Like a child asking their father not to let go of them in the deep end of the pool because they cannot swim. The new translation approved by some episcopal conferences —"do not let us succumb to temptation"— better reflects this meaning.

«Why seven petitions? Is it a magic number?»

Seven is the number of fullness in the Bible (the seven days of creation, the seven churches of Revelation, the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit). The seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer express the totality of what we need. Nothing is missing and nothing is superfluous. Saint Augustine says: "If you go through every petition, you will find nothing that is not contained in them." Seven is the number of completeness, of perfection.

Catechist's Prayer

Father of heaven,
who in seven words gave us everything:
make me a catechist of your holiest prayer.
May my students pass from reciting it
to dwelling in it,
from repeating it from memory
to praying it with the heart.
May I, in explaining each petition,
live it more deeply myself.
And may the idea they carry away today
not be a theory,
but a path to speak with you each day
in the words of your Son.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
